Sciences.title: Unit 1 – Energy in the Biosphere (Criterion A Practice)

questions.q: Which of the following BEST describes an ecosystem?
options: A group of animals living together in the same place
A system where living organisms interact only with each other
A community of living organisms interacting with each other and with their non-living environment
A collection of only animals that do not rely on their surroundings
questions.answer: A community of living organisms interacting with each other and with their non-living environment
questions.hint: An ecosystem includes BOTH biotic (living) AND abiotic (non-living) factors working together.

questions.q: Which of the following is an example of an ABIOTIC factor in an ecosystem?
questions.hint: Abiotic means NON-living. Think about which option is not a living organism.

questions.q: During photosynthesis, what raw materials do plants use?
questions.hint: Remember the equation: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂

questions.q: Which trophic level is responsible for entering energy from the Sun into the ecosystem?
questions.hint: Which organisms can make their own food using sunlight through photosynthesis?

questions.q: A grasshopper eats grass. A frog eats the grasshopper. What trophic level does the FROG occupy?
questions.hint: The frog eats an animal (grasshopper) that eats plants. Count the levels: grass → grasshopper → frog.

questions.q: In a trophic pyramid of energy, approximately what percentage of energy is transferred from one level to the next?
questions.hint: Most energy is lost through respiration, heat, movement, and waste at each level.

questions.q: Outline the difference between biotic and abiotic factors in an ecosystem. Give TWO examples of each and explain how they interact with each other.
questions.answer: Biotic factors are living components of an ecosystem, such as plants, animals, fungi, and bacteria. Abiotic factors are non-living components, such as sunlight, water, temperature, and soil. They interact because living organisms depend on non-living factors to survive: for example, plants (biotic) need sunlight and water (abiotic) to perform photosynthesis, and animals (biotic) need water (abiotic) to drink and air (abiotic) to breathe. If abiotic factors change (e.g. less water), biotic factors are affected (plants die, animals lose food).
questions.hint: Think about what 'living' vs 'non-living' means, and how they depend on each other.

questions.q: Outline the importance of producers (photosynthetic organisms) in an ecosystem. Include the photosynthesis equation and explain their role in the food chain.
questions.answer: Producers (plants, algae, some bacteria) are essential because they convert solar energy into chemical energy (glucose) through photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. They form the BASE of all food chains and food webs, providing food and energy for all consumers. Without producers, no energy from the Sun could enter the ecosystem. They also release oxygen needed for respiration and remove carbon dioxide from the atmosphere, helping maintain environmental balance and supporting biodiversity.
questions.hint: Think about where ALL energy in a food chain originally comes from, and what would happen without plants.

questions.q: Deforestation is destroying bird habitats. Suggest what would happen to the food chain if the bird population decreases significantly. Then suggest TWO solutions to protect birds.
questions.answer: If bird populations decrease: (1) Insects that birds eat would increase in number (population explosion), potentially becoming pests. (2) Predators that eat birds (like snakes or hawks) would lose a food source and their populations would also decline. (3) Plants that depend on birds for seed dispersal or pollination would be affected. The entire food web would become unbalanced. Solutions: (1) Create protected natural reserves where deforestation is prohibited. (2) Plant new trees and restore habitats to give birds places to nest.
questions.hint: Think step by step: what eats birds? What do birds eat? What happens when one link in the chain is removed?

questions.q: Interpret the following biomass pyramid data: Producers = 10,000 g/m², Primary Consumers = 1,000 g/m², Secondary Consumers = 100 g/m², Tertiary Consumers = 10 g/m². Describe the general trend, include the data to support it, and explain WHY producers are the most important level.
questions.answer: This is an upright biomass pyramid (terrestrial). The general trend shows that biomass DECREASES as you move up each trophic level. Producers have the most biomass at 10,000 g/m², which is 10 times more than primary consumers (1,000 g/m²), 100 times more than secondary consumers (100 g/m²), and 1,000 times more than tertiary consumers (10 g/m²). Only about 10% of biomass is transferred to the next level because energy is lost through respiration, heat, movement, incomplete digestion, and excretion. Producers are the most important level because they are the foundation — they capture solar energy through photosynthesis and make it available to the entire ecosystem. Without them, no other trophic level could exist.
questions.hint: Compare the numbers between each level. What is the ratio? Why does this happen? Think about energy loss.

Sciences.title: Unit 1 – Energy in the Biosphere (Practice Test 2)

questions.q: Which of the following is a TERRESTRIAL ecosystem?
questions.hint: Terrestrial means 'land-based'. Which of these ecosystems is found on land, not in water?

questions.q: What are the TWO main products of photosynthesis?
questions.hint: Look at the RIGHT side of the photosynthesis equation: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂

questions.q: Fungi and bacteria that break down dead organisms and return nutrients to the soil are called:
questions.hint: Which type of organism recycles nutrients from dead matter back into the ecosystem?

questions.q: What is the correct ORDER of organization from smallest to largest in biology?
questions.hint: Start with a single living thing, then a group of the same species, then different species together...

questions.q: An eagle eats a snake that eats a frog that eats insects that eat grass. What is the eagle in this food chain?
questions.hint: Count the levels: grass(1) → insect(2) → frog(3) → snake(4) → eagle(?). The eagle is at the top.

questions.q: What is the main difference between a food CHAIN and a food WEB?
options: A food chain only includes producers, a food web includes consumers
A food chain shows a single path of energy flow, a food web shows multiple interconnected paths
A food web only exists in aquatic ecosystems
A food chain is more accurate than a food web
questions.answer: A food chain shows a single path of energy flow, a food web shows multiple interconnected paths
questions.hint: Think about the word 'web' — what does a spider's web look like compared to a single chain?

questions.q: Outline why photosynthetic organisms are considered autotrophs. Explain the difference between autotrophs and heterotrophs, giving TWO examples of each.
questions.answer: Photosynthetic organisms are called autotrophs because they can produce their own food (chemical energy) using sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide through photosynthesis. 'Auto' means self and 'troph' means food — so autotrophs are self-feeders. Examples of autotrophs: plants and algae. Heterotrophs, on the other hand, cannot make their own food and must consume other organisms to obtain energy. 'Hetero' means other — so heterotrophs feed on others. Examples of heterotrophs: rabbits (herbivores that eat plants) and lions (carnivores that eat other animals). The key difference is that autotrophs produce energy from sunlight, while heterotrophs must eat other organisms.
questions.hint: Break down the words: 'auto' = self, 'hetero' = other, 'troph' = food/nutrition.

questions.q: A food chain in a forest ecosystem is: Oak tree → Caterpillar → Robin → Hawk. State the trophic level of EACH organism and outline what would happen to the ecosystem if a disease killed most of the caterpillars.
questions.answer: Oak tree = Producer (Trophic Level 1, autotroph). Caterpillar = Primary Consumer (Trophic Level 2, herbivore). Robin = Secondary Consumer (Trophic Level 3, carnivore/insectivore). Hawk = Tertiary Consumer (Trophic Level 4, apex predator). If a disease killed most caterpillars: (1) Oak trees would grow more because fewer caterpillars are eating their leaves. (2) Robins would lose their main food source, so their population would decrease or they would need to find alternative food. (3) Hawks that eat robins would also be affected because fewer robins means less food for them. (4) The entire food chain would become unbalanced, showing how removing one link affects all others.
questions.hint: Label each organism first, then think step by step: what happens ABOVE and BELOW the caterpillar in the chain?

questions.q: Explain why energy DECREASES as you move up the trophic pyramid. If producers capture 10,000 kJ of energy, calculate how much energy reaches the tertiary consumers. Use the 10% rule.
questions.answer: Energy decreases at each trophic level because organisms use most of the energy they consume for their own life processes: cellular respiration (to move, grow, and stay warm), incomplete digestion (not all food is digested), and excretion (waste products). Only about 10% of the energy at each level is passed to the next. Calculation using the 10% rule: Producers = 10,000 kJ → Primary Consumers = 10,000 × 10% = 1,000 kJ → Secondary Consumers = 1,000 × 10% = 100 kJ → Tertiary Consumers = 100 × 10% = 10 kJ. So tertiary consumers receive only 10 kJ out of the original 10,000 kJ — that is only 0.1% of the original energy! This explains why food chains rarely have more than 4-5 levels: there simply isn't enough energy left to support more levels.
questions.hint: At each level, multiply by 0.10 (10%). Do this three times starting from 10,000 kJ.

questions.q: Scientists discover that a lake ecosystem is losing its algae population due to pollution. Interpret how this would affect the entire aquatic food web. Include effects on at least THREE different trophic levels and suggest TWO actions humans could take to help restore the ecosystem.
questions.answer: Algae are the main producers in aquatic ecosystems. If algae disappear due to pollution: (1) PRODUCERS level: Less photosynthesis means less oxygen in the water and less chemical energy entering the food web. (2) PRIMARY CONSUMERS: Zooplankton and small fish that eat algae would lose their food source, causing their populations to decline. (3) SECONDARY CONSUMERS: Larger fish that eat zooplankton would also decrease because there is less food available. (4) TERTIARY CONSUMERS: Top predators like eagles or large fish would be affected as the entire food web collapses from the bottom up. Additionally, less photosynthesis means less oxygen dissolved in water, which could cause fish to suffocate. Actions to restore the ecosystem: (1) Reduce pollution by banning or limiting chemical dumping and pesticide runoff into the lake. (2) Create water treatment systems to filter pollutants before water enters the lake. These actions would allow algae populations to recover and rebuild the food web from the base up.
questions.hint: Start from the bottom (algae = producers) and work your way UP the food web. Think about oxygen too!
